attributes.stem:
A 35-year-old woman is admitted to hospital at 39 weeks gestation for an elective caesarean section for breech presentation. This will be her third child. She requests permanent sterilisation at the time of caesarean section. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. Her previous pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. She is in a stable relationship, and both she and her partner are certain they do not want more children.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The fetus is in frank breech position with normal cardiotocography.

The surgeon plans to perform a tubal ligation with modified Pomeroy technique where a portion of each Fallopian tube is cut and removed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most accurate statement regarding tubal ligation outcomes to discuss with this patient?
options: Pregnancy is impossible after tubal ligation
The lifetime risk of pregnancy is approximately 1 in 200
Reversal success rates are over 90% if desired in future
The risk of ectopic pregnancy is higher than intrauterine pregnancy
Success rates of reversal are independent of maternal age
attributes.explanation:
When counselling patients about permanent sterilisation, it is important to provide accurate information about both the failure rate and the possibility of future reversal. This allows for informed decision-making, particularly in younger women who may have a higher likelihood of requesting reversal in the future.

The lifetime risk of pregnancy is approximately 1 in 200 after tubal ligation. This risk is higher in women who undergo the procedure at a younger age, possibly due to increased fertility potential and a longer reproductive lifespan. The method of occlusion can also affect failure rates, with clips having a slightly higher failure rate than complete surgical division.

It is incorrect to state that pregnancy is impossible after tubal ligation. While the procedure is highly effective, no method of contraception is 100% reliable. Failures can occur due to incomplete occlusion, spontaneous recanalisation, or fistula formation.

Reversal success rates are over 90% is an overestimate. The success rates for tubal re-anastomosis vary significantly, ranging from 40-85%, depending on various factors including the type of original sterilisation procedure, the length of remaining tube, and most importantly, maternal age.

While the risk of ectopic pregnancy is increased relative to the general population if pregnancy occurs after tubal ligation, the absolute risk of intrauterine pregnancy is still higher than ectopic pregnancy in cases of sterilisation failure.

Success rates of reversal are significantly affected by maternal age. Women under 35 years have the highest success rates (60-85%), while those over 40 have much lower success rates (30-40%). This information is particularly relevant for younger women considering the procedure.
learningPoints: Tubal ligation failure rates are approximately 1 in 200 over a lifetime.
The risk of failure is highest in women who undergo the procedure at a young age.
Tubal re-anastomosis has a pregnancy success rate of 40-85%, with success rates decreasing with increasing maternal age.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Contraception Clinical Guideline [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2023 [cited 2024 Nov 30]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Contraception-Clinical-Guideline.pdf
